threats.title: Governance Bypass: Defeated and Expired Proposals Can Be Executed
threats.hypothesis_description: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol execute() functions allow execution when proposal state is Succeeded, Defeated, or Expired. Any user can submit a malicious proposal, let it be voted down or expire, and still execute the arbitrary payload. This completely nullifies the governance voting mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement explicitly includes ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired alongside ProposalState.Succeeded, meaning every proposal that completes its voting lifecycle is executable regardless of outcome.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)

preconditions: proposalThreshold() returns 0 (any voter can propose)
Attacker has at least 0 voting power (threshold is 0)
Voting period has elapsed so proposal reaches Defeated/Expired state

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: execute() state check explicitly permits Defeated and Expired states alongside Succeeded
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action required; any caller can execute any completed proposal
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After voting period ends, any defeated/expired proposal is permanently executable

operational_questions: Are there any external safeguards (timelock, multisig) wrapping the Governor that would block execution?
What contracts/functions can the Governor execute actions on?
Is relay() or onlyGovernance-protected functions reachable via this path?
validation_requirements: Create proposal with malicious targets, vote against, call execute after deadline
Verify _execute() loops through targets and executes arbitrary calls

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit governance proposal with malicious calldata targeting protocol treasury or critical config
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for voting period to end (proposal gets Defeated or Expired due to no/low votes)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call execute() with the proposal parameters - passes the Defeated/Expired state check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Malicious payload executes: drain treasury, change quorum to 0, update critical parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single attacker, single transaction after voting period

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete governance takeover - any proposed action is executable
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All protocol parameters controlled by governance are compromised
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All onlyGovernance-protected functions including relay(), updateQuorumNumerator()

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after a proposal's voting period ends

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Any execute() call on a non-Succeeded proposal
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals created with suspicious target addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proposals submitted by unknown addresses with no community engagement

threats.title: Fake Genesis Pool Injection via Unvalidated depositToken Parameter
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() accepts any non-zero address as genesisPool without validating it was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker deploys a malicious contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken(), triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a gauge for an attacker-controlled pair address, potentially redirecting protocol emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function takes genesisPool as a parameter and calls external functions on it without verifying its origin from the factory. The only checks are amount > 0 and genesisPool != address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: No validation that genesisPool was created by genesisFactory before calling IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken()

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
gaugeManager.createGauge() creates gauge for any pair address
tokenHandler.whitelistToken() is callable from the manager

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: depositToken trusts caller-provided genesisPool address without factory verification
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin/governance involvement needed - any external caller can trigger
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - the validation gap exists for any call

operational_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge validate the pair address against the pair factory?
Can emissions be redirected to the unauthorized gauge?
Does the fake pool's depositToken need to handle actual token transfers?
validation_requirements: Deploy fake IGenesisPool contract returning controlled values
Call depositToken with fake pool, verify gauge creation
Check if emissions can be claimed from unauthorized gauge

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy FakeGenesisPool contract: depositToken returns true, getGenesisInfo returns controlled nativeToken, getLiquidityPoolInfo returns attacker's pair
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager calls _preLaunchPool which creates gauge via gaugeManager.createGauge for attacker's pair
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker receives gauge/bribe addresses for an unauthorized pool, potentially capturing emissions

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Unauthorized gauge creation bypassing governance approval
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol emissions potentially redirected to attacker-controlled pools
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires killing unauthorized gauges and patching validation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Gauge creation, emission distribution, token whitelisting

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs to implement IGenesisPool interface

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: depositToken calls with genesisPool addresses not in factory
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Gauge creation for unknown pair addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: New contracts deployed implementing IGenesisPool interface

threats.title: setRouter Inverted Check Permanently Bricks Router Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter() contains require(_router == address(0)) instead of require(_router != address(0)). This means the only value that passes validation is address(0). If the owner calls this function, the router is set to address(0), permanently bricking all genesis pool launches since launch() calls IRouter(address(0)).addLiquidity() which reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require condition is logically inverted compared to standard zero-address validation patterns. The error string 'ZA' suggests the developer intended to prevent zero-address, but the condition permits only zero-address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_router == address(0), 'ZA') instead of require(_router != address(0), 'ZA')

preconditions: Owner calls setRouter() with any address
If called with address(0), all future launches are bricked
If called with valid address, function reverts and router cannot be updated

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: require(_router == address(0)) is logically opposite of defensive zero-address check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must call the function for immediate impact; inability to update router is the latent risk
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent code defect in setter function

operational_questions: Has setRouter() ever been called on the deployed contract?
Is the current router address functional and does it need updating?
Are there any other mechanisms to update the router address?
validation_requirements: Call setRouter(validAddress) and confirm revert
Call setRouter(address(0)) and confirm it succeeds
Verify launch() reverts after router is set to address(0)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe that setRouter only accepts address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If owner calls setRouter(address(0)), router becomes zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All checkAtEpochFlip -> _launchPool -> launch() calls revert because IRouter(address(0)).addLiquidity fails
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All genesis pools in PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED state are permanently stuck, user funds locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires owner to call the function (or recognize inability to update router)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Router address cannot be legitimately updated
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: If set to zero, all genesis pool launches permanently fail
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade via proxy pattern
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All genesis pool launches, liquidity provisioning

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time owner attempts to update router

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setRouter() call with address(0) on the proxy
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Launch failures for all genesis pools simultaneously
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failed setRouter calls with valid addresses

threats.title: Epoch Flip DoS: Single Failing Genesis Pool Blocks All Pool Transitions
threats.hypothesis_description: checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens and make external calls for each pool without try-catch. If any single pool's external call reverts (e.g., pairFactory.setGenesisStatus, gaugeManager.createGauge, or IGenesisPool.launch), the entire transaction reverts, blocking all other genesis pools from transitioning states.
threats.investigation_rationale: Both functions loop through all live tokens making external calls. A single revert causes the entire batch to fail. pre_epoch_period is only updated after the loop in checkBeforeEpochFlip, so repeated failures also prevent epoch advancement.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop over liveNativeTokens calling external functions without try-catch; single revert blocks all

preconditions: At least one genesis pool in liveNativeTokens has a condition that causes revert
epochController calls checkAtEpochFlip or checkBeforeEpochFlip
External call (pairFactory, gaugeManager, or genesisPool) reverts for any pool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No error isolation between pool processing - single failure cascades to all
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Can be triggered by any pool with problematic token (fee-on-transfer, paused, etc.)

operational_questions: Can an attacker intentionally create a genesis pool that will revert during launch?
Are there admin recovery mechanisms to remove problematic pools?
Does rejectGenesisPool remove from liveNativeTokens to unblock?
validation_requirements: Create pool with token that reverts on approve/transfer
Verify checkAtEpochFlip reverts for all pools when one fails
Test governance's ability to remove the blocking pool

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Get whitelisted and create genesis pool with a token that will revert during addLiquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: checkAtEpochFlip attempts launch, reverts on malicious pool, blocks all pools
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All legitimate genesis pools stuck, depositors' funds locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires whitelist access or governance bypass

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All genesis pools stuck in current state, funds temporarily locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Governance must reject the problematic pool to unblock

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs to understand token mechanics

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Pools created with unusual or malicious tokens

threats.title: Quorum Manipulation via Non-Snapshot Total Supply in Governor.sol
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction, the quorum() function calls token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp parameter. This uses current total supply rather than historical supply at proposal snapshot time, allowing quorum threshold to be manipulated after proposal creation by changing the smNFT total supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The quorum function signature accepts blockTimestamp but the implementation ignores it, using a parameterless call to getsmNFTPastTotalSupply(). This breaks the snapshot-based quorum guarantee.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return (token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator() -- blockTimestamp parameter ignored

preconditions: Governor.sol variant (smNFT-based) is deployed and active
smNFT total supply can change between proposal creation and execution check
Quorum check happens at execution time using current supply

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: quorum() ignores blockTimestamp, always using current total supply
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action needed - supply changes from normal protocol operations affect quorum
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between proposal creation and execution state evaluation

operational_questions: How volatile is smNFT total supply between epochs?
Can an attacker cause significant supply changes to manipulate quorum?
Does this affect the L2Governor.sol variant as well?
validation_requirements: Compare quorum(timestamp) return values at different supply levels
Verify getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() behavior without timestamp

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create proposal when smNFT supply is at a known level
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cause supply decrease (or wait for natural decrease) to lower quorum threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Minimal votes now meet the lowered quorum requirement
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Proposal that shouldn't pass quorum is now executable (combined with OPS-H001, even defeated proposals execute)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Ability to influence smNFT total supply

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Quorum threshold manipulation undermines governance security
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Proposals pass with insufficient community participation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix quorum calculation

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Enough to influence total supply direction
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires timing with supply changes
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Between proposal creation and vote deadline

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Significant smNFT supply changes during active proposal periods
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals passing quorum with unusually low vote counts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large smNFT minting/burning around proposal deadlines

threats.title: Zero Slippage Protection in addLiquidity Enables Pair Price Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool._addLiquidityAndDistribute() calls IRouter.addLiquidity with amountAMin=0 and amountBMin=0. Since genesis pool launches are predictable (triggered by epoch controller), an attacker can manipulate the pair's reserves before the launch by directly transferring tokens to the pair contract, causing the liquidity to be added at a skewed ratio and extracting value through arbitrage.
threats.investigation_rationale: The addLiquidity call passes (0, 0) for minimum amounts, accepting any price ratio. The launch timing is deterministic and observable on-chain. While front-running is not possible on this chain, the pair manipulation can be done in a prior block.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IRouter(_router).addLiquidity(..., 0, 0, address(this), block.timestamp + 100) -- zero min amounts

preconditions: Pair address is known after approveGenesisPool
setGenesisStatus(true) does not prevent direct token transfers to pair
Attacker can transfer tokens to pair and call sync() before launch
Launch timing is predictable from epoch schedule

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: addLiquidity with (0,0) min amounts accepts any price manipulation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - exploit occurs during automated epoch flip launch
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between pool approval and launch execution

operational_questions: Does setGenesisStatus prevent direct token transfers and sync on the pair?
Can the pair reserves be manipulated before launch despite genesis status?
How much value can be extracted through the price manipulation?
validation_requirements: Test whether tokens can be transferred to genesis-status pair
Verify if sync() works on a genesis-protected pair
Calculate LP token loss from price manipulation scenarios

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify pair address from approveGenesisPool event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transfer tokens to pair to skew reserves before epoch flip launch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Launch executes addLiquidity with 0 slippage, accepting skewed ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker arbitrages the mispriced LP to extract value
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single attacker, requires tokens of the pair

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Genesis pool participants receive less value in LP tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Value already extracted, LP tokens permanently undervalued

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before epoch flip that triggers launch

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Direct token transfers to genesis pairs before launch
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unusual reserves in pair at launch time

threats.title: Abstain Vote Weaponization Combined With Defeated Proposal Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorCountingSimple, _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes AND forVotes > abstainVotes. Abstain votes can be used to block legitimate proposals from reaching Succeeded state even with majority For support. Combined with OPS-H001 (execute allows Defeated/Expired), the proposal ends up in Expired state (quorum met but neither succeeded nor defeated) and is still executable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _voteSucceeded function in Governor.sol has a stricter condition than standard governance: forVotes must exceed BOTH againstVotes AND abstainVotes. This deviates from standard OpenZeppelin where only forVotes > againstVotes is needed.
preconditions: Governor.sol (smNFT-based) variant is deployed
Attacker has enough voting power to cast significant Abstain votes
Proposal has quorum met via For + Abstain votes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > abstainVotes, allowing abstain to block legitimate proposals

operational_questions: What is the typical ratio of For:Against:Abstain in historical proposals?
How much voting power is needed to weaponize Abstain?
Does the L2Governor.sol variant have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Create proposal, vote with more Abstain than For, verify Expired state
Verify execute() succeeds on Expired state proposal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit legitimate-looking proposal to gain community For votes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cast large Abstain vote exceeding For votes using attacker's smNFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Proposal expires (quorum met, but neither succeeded nor defeated)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If attacker wants: execute the Expired proposal via OPS-H001 bug
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Sufficient smNFT voting power for Abstain votes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol unable to make governance changes

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Enough smNFTs to outvote For tallies with Abstain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large Abstain votes exceeding For votes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Coordinated Abstain voting from multiple addresses

threats.title: Incentives Not Zeroed After Transfer Creates Stuck Claims on Status Regression
threats.hypothesis_description: When transferIncentives() sends incentive tokens to the external bribe via notifyRewardAmount, the incentives mapping retains non-zero values. If setPoolStatus later transitions the pool to NOT_QUALIFIED, claimIncentives() will attempt to transfer tokens the pool no longer holds, reverting on every claim attempt. The token owner sees claimable amounts but can never claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: transferIncentives does not zero out the incentives mapping after sending tokens. The incentives mapping is only zeroed in claimIncentives. A status regression to NOT_QUALIFIED after transfer creates an irrecoverable state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: incentives[token] values not zeroed after safeApprove and notifyRewardAmount; claimIncentives reads stale values

preconditions: Pool has incentives added and is in PRE_LAUNCH state (incentives already transferred)
Manager calls setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED) on the pool
Token owner attempts to claim incentives

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferIncentives sends tokens but leaves incentives mapping non-zero
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must set status to NOT_QUALIFIED after incentive transfer for bug to manifest
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After transferIncentives but pool fails to launch

operational_questions: Can setPoolStatus be called to regress from PRE_LAUNCH to NOT_QUALIFIED?
Are there operational scenarios where this transition happens naturally?
What is the value of incentives typically locked in this scenario?
validation_requirements: Advance pool to PRE_LAUNCH, then set NOT_QUALIFIED, attempt claimIncentives
Verify claimIncentives reverts due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Token owner deposits native token and adds incentives
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Pool advances to PRE_LAUNCH (incentives transferred to bribe)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Governance or manager sets pool to NOT_QUALIFIED
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token owner calls claimIncentives - reverts because contract has no balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance/manager action to regress pool status

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token owner permanently loses incentive tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens sent to bribe contract - may not be recoverable

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When pool is disqualified after pre-launch

threats.title: Null Auction Address Bricks Genesis Pool Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: In depositNativeToken(), if auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex) returns address(0) and the fallback auctions(0) also returns address(0) (both removed), the auction is set to address(0) in the genesis pool. All subsequent depositToken calls revert because _getNativeTokenAmount calls IAuction(address(0)).getNativeTokenAmount which reverts at a zero address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The auction selection logic falls back to auctions(0) when the specified index returns zero, but doesn't validate the fallback result. If the admin removes the auction at index 0, the fallback also returns address(0).
deployment_assumptions: AuctionFactory auction at index 0 could be removed via removeAuction(0)
threats.config_evidence: AuctionFactory.removeAuction() sets auctions[_pos] = address(0); no protection against removing index 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: auction = auction == address(0) ? auctionFactory.auctions(0) : auction; -- no validation result is non-zero

preconditions: AuctionFactory owner removes auction at index 0
User calls depositNativeToken with auctionIndex pointing to removed auction
Both primary and fallback auction addresses are address(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No validation that resolved auction address is non-zero before setting on genesis pool
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must remove default auction for condition to trigger

operational_questions: Has removeAuction ever been called on index 0?
Is there validation in setGenesisPoolInfo for zero auction?
Can the genesis pool recover via setAuction?
validation_requirements: Remove auction at index 0, call depositNativeToken, verify pool is bricked
Verify setAuction can fix the pool after creation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: AuctionFactory owner removes auction at index 0 (operational mistake)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User creates genesis pool - auction set to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Genesis pool is permanently bricked unless setAuction is called to fix it

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Affected genesis pools cannot accept deposits

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: depositToken reverts for new genesis pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Genesis pools created with zero auction address

threats.title: Thenian NFT Reentrancy via _safeMint Bypasses Per-Transaction Mint Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: Thenian mintPublic(), mintFirst(), and mintSecond() use _safeMint which triggers onERC721Received callback on contract recipients. A malicious contract can re-enter the mint functions during the callback to mint additional NFTs beyond the per-transaction MAX_PER_MINT limit of 10, though still bounded by the per-wallet limit of 15.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _mintTo function calls _safeMint in a loop. Each _safeMint triggers a callback where the attacker can re-enter. The per-wallet checks use balanceOf which reflects current state, so the 15 per-wallet cap still holds, but the 10 per-tx limit is bypassable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeMint(account, totalSupply()) triggers onERC721Received callback; no ReentrancyGuard on mint functions

preconditions: Attacker deploys contract with onERC721Received that re-enters mintPublic
Public sale is active (after SALE_START_TIMESTAMP + 2 days)
Attacker contract sends correct msg.value on re-entry

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _safeMint callback allows re-entry; no nonReentrant modifier on mint functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any user during public sale

operational_questions: Is the per-wallet limit of 15 sufficient protection?
Can the attacker deploy multiple contracts to exceed 15?
Does originalMinters tracking affect any downstream logic?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that re-enters mintPublic during onERC721Received
Verify more than 10 mints in single outer transaction

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy contract with onERC721Received that calls mintPublic() with correct value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During _safeMint callback, re-enter mintPublic(1) up to wallet limit of 15
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Mint 15 NFTs in single transaction instead of max 10

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Per-transaction limit bypassed; faster supply exhaustion
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: NFTs already minted cannot be recovered

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs to implement ERC721 receiver with re-entry

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract addresses minting NFTs; multiple mints in single tx
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: More than 10 mints in single transaction from same address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Contracts calling mintPublic instead of EOAs

threats.title: Division by Zero in balanceOf When totalDeposits Becomes Zero After Launch
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.balanceOf() computes balance = (_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits. After launch, if deductAllAmount is called for all depositors (setting their userDeposits to 0), totalDeposits remains unchanged but all individual deposits are zero. More critically, if the gauge integration leads to totalDeposits being reduced to 0 through deductAmount calls, any subsequent balanceOf query divides by zero and reverts, breaking external integrations.
threats.investigation_rationale: deductAmount reduces userDeposits[account] but the relationship between userDeposits reductions and totalDeposits is not maintained in all code paths. deductAllAmount sets userDeposits to 0 but does not reduce totalDeposits. This inconsistency could cause accounting issues.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balance = (_depositerLiquidity * userDeposits[account]) / totalDeposits -- division by totalDeposits with no zero check

preconditions: Pool has launched (liquidity > 0)
totalDeposits has been reduced to 0 through deduct operations or is structurally 0
External contract or user queries balanceOf for any non-owner account

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No zero-check on totalDeposits before division in balanceOf
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Gauge calls deductAmount/deductAllAmount during normal unstaking
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After all depositors have fully unstaked from gauge

operational_questions: Does deductAmount properly reduce totalDeposits alongside userDeposits?
Does deductAllAmount leave totalDeposits inconsistent?
What external contracts call balanceOf and could be affected by revert?
validation_requirements: Launch pool, have all depositors unstake via gauge, query balanceOf
Verify deductAllAmount does not update totalDeposits

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Pool launches with deposits from multiple users
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All users unstake from gauge, triggering deductAllAmount for each
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: userDeposits are zeroed but totalDeposits may not be properly updated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Any balanceOf query reverts with division by zero if totalDeposits reaches 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Natural operational flow - all depositors unstaking

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: balanceOf reverts break external integrations relying on it
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade - no way to fix totalDeposits
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Balance queries, gauge interactions, any UI showing balances

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverting balanceOf calls on launched genesis pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: All depositors unstaking from same pool
